data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: It's Too Early To Get Excited About siRNA Data
data.description:
Investment Overview

Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) stock made a 35% gain yesterday, rising to a value of $34 per share (market cap of $2.5bn), after the company shared the first clinical results from two of its siRNA programs for neuromuscular diseases, SRP-001, indicated for facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy type 1 ("FSHD1"), and SRP-1003, indicated for myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). In early trading today, shares are down ~7%.

Sarepta's short, interfering RNA ("siRNA") programs were developed using Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) targeted RNAi molecule ("TrIM") platform which is, according to Sarepta's 2025 annual report / 10K filing:

designed to deliver siRNA to multiple tissue and cell types throughout the body with the goal of initiating the RNA interference mechanism and inducing rapid and durable knockdown of target genes.

An overview of the platform is also provided in the slide below, taken from Sarepta's results presentation, shared yesterday:

Overview Of SRP-1001 Results

In a press release also shared yesterday Sarepta notes that FSHD is a rare genetic disease that causes progressive weakness of skeletal muscles:

caused by abnormal activation of the DUX4 gene on chromosome 4, leading to production of the DUX4 protein, which is toxic to muscle cells and drives muscle degeneration.

There are no cures or disease modifying therapies for the condition approved today, the company also notes. SRP-1001 is designed to reduce or "knock down" the production of DUX4 protein in patients' skeletal muscle.

Sarepta's Phase 1/2 randomised study involved single and multiple ascending dose ("SAD / MAD") cohorts, and enrolled 56 patients in total, 40 treated with varying doses of SRP-001 (up to a maximum 8.17mg/kg dose), and 10 treated with placebo.

Among the highlights from the study shared yesterday, Sarepta noted that:

  • a single dose of SRP-1001 delivers 6-fold greater siRNA muscle concentration than multiple doses of transferrin-targeting ligand and represents the highest siRNA dose with muscle PK data to date;
  • a single dose of SRP-1001 is providing suppression equal to or in excess of 90% across all 3 panels when adjusted to placebo - Sarepta says this represents the greatest DUX4 gene reduction in the field observed to date;
  • SRP-1001 drives rapid reduction of serum creatine kinase ("CK") with a single dose.
  • The safety profile is described as "reassuring", with no serious related treatment emergent adverse events ("TEAEs" reported). 77% of patients experienced TEAEs, and one patient experienced a serious TEAE deemed not to be study related.

Overview Of SRP-1003 Results

DM1 is a common form of adult-onset muscular dystrophy caused by repeat expansion in DPMK gene, Sarepta says, that affects skeletal and smooth muscle, and potentially the heart, respiratory system, eye, and endocrine, gastrointestinal and central nervous systems. It is a ~40k patient population in the US, with no cures or disease modifying therapies currently available.

SRP-1003 is designed to target or "knock-down" the DMPK messenger-RNA in target cells, halting the production of the disease causing protein.

The Phase 1/2 study was again a SAD/MAD study involving a placebo arm, with 36 patients treated with SRP-1003 across four cohorts, up to a maximum dose of 4.1mg/kg, and 11 patients receiving placebo.

The main highlights Sarepta shared yesterday included a dose dependent increase in plasma exposure, superior muscle delivery even with single low dose, and a "reassuring safety profile, although three serious AEs were reported in two patients - fatal arrhythmia, limb abscesses, and emotional distress, deemed unrelated to the study drug. 77.8% of patients experienced a TEAE.

Analysis - Sarepta Looking To Bounce Back After A Tough 2025

At the beginning of 2025, Sarepta was looking forward with confidence - as I explained in a note for Seeking Alpha shared in January last year, the company was projecting for ~$3bn of revenues in 2025, led by its gene therapy for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD") therapy Elevidys, which management believed would experience the most successful commercial launch of any gene therapy ever.

That wasn't to be the case however. Sarepta stock crashed in value and as I wrote in note on the company in October last year (assigning a Hold rating, following a Sell rating I assigned in July last year):

In Q1 2025, Sarepta reported Elevidys revenues of $375m, and total revenues of $612m, but the company had disclosed in March that a patient receiving treatment with Elevidys had died from acute liver failure ("ALF"), and in June, it reported that a second death had occurred, also from ALF. In July, Sarepta reported the death of a third patient, this time of a non-ambulatory limb-girdle muscular dystrophy ("LGMD") patient participating in a Phase 1/2 trial for Sarepta's gene therapy candidate SRP-9004.

Sarepta suspended all commercial shipments of ELEVIDYS to non-ambulatory patients and voluntarily stopped shipments of the drug to ambulatory patients, although it has since resumed the latter, in agreement with the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA").

As per Sarepta's 2025 annual report / 10K filing:

The Company recently announced the conclusion of the label supplement for ELEVIDYS, including the addition of a boxed warning for risk of ALI and ALF and the removal of the non-ambulatory population from the Indication and Usage section.

Elevidys - marketed and sold with Pharma giant Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) - in fact failed to meet the primary efficacy endpoint in its pivotal EMBARK study, although it did demonstrate clinically meaningful and statistically significant benefits in both key secondary functional endpoints: time to rise from floor and 10 minute walk/run test, which was enough to secure a full approval from the FDA for ambulatory patients.

Also last year, Sarepta reported that two of its EXON skipping drugs developed to treat DMD, Vyondys 53 and Amondys 45, which had been granted an accelerated approval (approval based on a biomarker indicative of a clinical benefit, but not itself a measure of clinical benefit) in 2019 and 2021, had failed to meet the primary endpoint of a post-confirmatory study.

In the end, Sarepta announced full-year 2025 revenues of $1.86bn, with Elevidys contributing $898.7m of revenues, and its exon-skipping products $965.6m of revenues. Total revenues were reported as $2.2bn, with a GAAP net loss of $(713m), of $(7.13) per share. Last July, the company announced a corporate restructuring in July last year, which may see up to 36% of the workforce depart. In February this year, Sarepta's CEO Doug Ingram also announced his retirement from the company.

Sarepta also reported ~$1bn in cash as of the end of 2025, and says it anticipates remaining profitable and cash‑flow positive in 2026. The company is projecting $1.2bn - $1.4bn of revenues for Elevidys in 2026.

As we can see above, there are many data readouts and business updates planned in the next 12-18 months. Elevidys will read out more data, Sarepta will update on the outcome of an FDA meeting to discuss the confirmatory Vyondys / Amondys study data and a Huntington's Disease therapy will enter clinical studies. As CEO Ingram discussed on yesterday's call:

To remind you, we have 5 programs in clinical development right now. We have FSHD and DM1. We have IPF, SCA2 and Huntington's disease. We have 2 other programs in animal testing, and we have 6 research programs that make up this portfolio of siRNA. Our 5 clinical programs alone offer potential therapy to as many as 160,000 patients in the United States and multiples greater than that worldwide.

Analysis - Will Yesterday's Data Spark A Long Term Rise In Sarepta Stock?

I think there are essentially two ways of looking at Sarepta's business today - a bullish and a bearish. Let's start with the bearish.

Ultimately, Sarepta may have four drug products on the market, all indicated for DMD, but three of these have failed to meet key primary endpoints in key studies, and there is a risk that the FDA could ask the company to take Vyondys and Exondys off the market this year. Fears around prescribing Elevidys due to safety concerns could also undermine that products' sales going forward.

Meanwhile, there are many current and future competitors in the DMD space. Japanses Pharma Nippon Shinyaku has an exon 53 skipping therapy, Viltepso on the market, and is developing two products to treat patients living with Duchenne that have mutations amenable to exons 44 and 50, respectively.

Italfarmaco also has an approved DMD product, Givinostat, and the list of pharmas and biotechs developing DMD therapies includes Wave Life Sciences (WVE), Dyne Therapeutics (DYN), Avidity Biosciences (RNA), Entrada Therapeutics (TRDA), Regenxbio (RGNX), Biomarin (BMRN), Solid Biosciences (SLDB), Insmed (INSD), and Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR).

Sarepta made a loss of <$(700m) last year, and has only ever been profitable in 2024, having typically lost >$(500m) per annum in prior years as far back as 2018. In summary, the bear case is built on products with limited treatment effect, heavy losses, intense competition, safety issues, lack of forward guidance / falling revenues, and the departure of a long-serving CEO.

If that paints the picture of a company in crisis, let's consider the bull case.

This is built on the fact that while Sarepta's therapies may not have met certain endpoints, they did meet others, and are arguably the best available therapies that have treated patients with few or no other viable treatment options available for many years, resulting in - anecdotally at least - significant improvements in many patients.

Elevidys remains on the market, with "blockbuster" revenues forecast for 2026, and the company is advancing new therapies to market, which have demonstrated some intriguing, albeit early stage data. We might therefore speculate that a market cap of $2.3bn for a company promising revenues in the billions, profitability and to be cash flow positive in 2026 is too low.

How does the data shared yesterday change the picture? For me, the issue that dogs Sarepta - and most other companies developing therapies for muscle wasting diseases - is the inability to generate truly statistically significant efficacy in patients, instead relying on biomarker data suggestive of benefit to secure approvals for drugs that command - in Elevidys' case - multi-million dollar list prices.

I consider yesterday's data to be more of the same - yes, there are signs that the therapy is reaching the target cells, and generating some promising biomarker data, but will that ultimately result in the drug's ability to meaningfully improve patients conditions - I think that is hard to state with any real conviction, as former encouraging biomarker data has arguably not translated to positive treatment outcomes.

It's a difficult subject - some benefit is arguably better than no benefit, but when we consider TEAEs, serious TEAS, and costs, and the inability to meet a primary endpoint in a pivotal study - is it even possible to make that argument?

As such, I would caution against reading too much into yesterday's data and while I wish Sarepta the best of luck with its studies, I'd be firmly on the side-lines as regards the investment opportunity here. There have been too many false dawns, and late stage efficacy data is what truly matters.

data.title: Nike At 5-Year Lows: Why The Turnaround Is Already Working
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Investment Call: Strong Buy

The Nike, Inc. (NYSE: NKE) de-rating was among the worst in the history of the large-cap consumer brands. In early 2021, it hit an all-time high of about $179 but in March of 2026, it was down over 70% to about $53 per share, burdened by a tariff headwind worth about $1.5 billion per year and the price of a complete operational reset. This is the first time Nike has not traded at these levels since 2017. Nike has unmatched brand awareness, despite these headwinds, ranked #1 in unaided consumer awareness in athletic footwear and apparel worldwide. The offer by Elliott Hill to restructure the company in a systematic way is showing a positive curve in various operational indicators. Nike is not a structurally handicapped organization but a well-capitalized franchise with a planned re-set to sustainable long-run profitability. The 12-month price forecast of $85 is a pointer to mid-cycle EBIT revival and low multiple re-rating as the "Win Now" strategy gains traction.

The investment thesis is based on five components that are interconnected. Marketing spend is projected to exceed $5 billion in FY2026 according to LSEG estimates, and Nike has no fewer athletic partnerships than Michael Jordan, LeBron James, Cristiano Ronaldo, Serena Williams and hundreds more in every major sport. Although On, Hoka, and New Balance have achieved considerable gains in some segments, it would be a major challenge for any new or current rival to replicate over 50 years of brand heritage and global association with athletes. The new CEO Elliott Hill has 32 years of experience in business development and retail relationships at Nike before retirement in 2020. The first thing Hill has done to fill the leadership void left by John Donahoe is to recover wholesale as the foundation of the distribution economics of Nike and salvage the relationships that were destroyed. The channel also showed evident progress: according to the Q2 FY2026 release, wholesale revenues increased by 8% in comparison with the previous year to $7.5 billion, and North American wholesale increased +24%. Nike's running division—its most strategically important sport segment—has maintained growth of more than 20% in two quarters straight, which is real brand momentum as opposed to in-promotion response. NKE shares are currently priced at around 22x FY2027E consensus EPS of $2.43 versus the market which is remaining cautious to the point of future Q3 FY2026 earnings release at its historical mid-cycle multiple of about 22x.

Q2 FY2026 Results: The Beat the Market Couldn't Celebrate

The Nike FY2026 fiscal second quarter expectations, released on December 18, 2025, showed a strong difference between investor perception and business trajectory. Its diluted Nike EPS is also priced at $0.53, which is roughly 43 percent above the estimated price of $0.37. It is the first time that the full year revenue of the company is compared positively after many years of decline to the single digit level of $12.4 billion. The 2nd consecutive quarter revealed a growth of North American revenue by 9% and a growth of the global wholesale revenue by 8%.The running business increased by over 20%. The stock declined about 11% in the following days after the announcement as the investors paid attention to the gross margin shrinkage of 300 basis points to 40.6, a result of the annual tariffs and inventory obsolete costs (1.5 billion). The management showed caution about low-single-digit revenue depreciations in Q3.

The shrinkage of the margin can be attributed to two transient variables. The management is also striving to mix the shift of the fiscal year 2026 and the pricing endeavors to counteract tariff headwinds. The gross tariff headwind in FY2026 would be about 315 basis points to gross margin, as was mentioned in the Q2 earnings call, and it is projected that the net effect of mitigation measures will reduce it to about 120 basis points. Hill has reduced channel legacy styles that were operating on below-average margins. The point in inventory liquidation charges by Hill is to release available shelf space and give the consumer a sampler of the new products they are offering at their retail price. In the earnings call, CFO Matt Friend pointed to the fact that the lack of tariffs would have seen costs increase, and powertrain operations enjoyed the result of long lead times and sales to push down prices.

There is also high strategic flexibility in the Nike balance sheet. As of November 30, 2025, they had liquid assets of $8.35 billion in the form of $6.97 billion and $1.37 billion of cash and short-term investments, respectively, and a market value of approximately $82 billion. As the Nike IR announcement shows, the current ratio of 2.1 reveals that there is no short-term liquidity pressure and no pressure to refinance in the near future. In November 2025, the board announced a quarterly dividend of $0.41, or 3% higher than the previous dividend of $0.40 ($1.64 per annum), and this is the 24th consecutive annual dividend increase. The free cash flow is the only way the company can attain the dividend of $1.64 billion. Besides the shares that were bought by the CEO, Elliott Hill, the data assembled by the board chairman of Nike, Tim Cook, shows that the most knowledgeable individuals regarding the future of the company are making the most of their own work.

The "Win Now" Strategy: Five Pillars, Measurable Progress

The five pillars of the "Win Now" program, i.e. culture, product innovation, marketing investment, marketplace rebalancing, and in-person experience, also referred to in FY2025 Q3 of the earnings call by Elliott Hill. The strategy represents a premeditated course of correction of the course followed by the previous management, literally overturning a number of decisions made in 2020–2024. The business strategy is reformulated at Nike: the business strategy switched into de-emphasizing its wholesale partners, replacing brand marketing with performance marketing, and Nike was forced off Amazon to reduce its costs and the number of wholesale partners. The promotion was detracting from the brand equity and favoritism to the competition, such as Hoka and New Balance, with regard to incremental floor space and attention, which is at the cost of brand equity. The departure of Nike from Amazon predetermines the withdrawal from the most popular online discovery and purchase platform in the US.

But Hill has already changed their minds, and there has been a resounding victory. Nike returning to the Amazon marketplace has revitalized the attention of the approximately 38% of American consumers that mainly use the site to search and buy sporting footwear. In September 2025, Dick's Sporting Goods acquired Foot Locker, creating a massive wholesale partner that served about $5 billion of the annual Nike profits, and thus the wholesale strategy became easier due to the inclusion of bigger and more committed wholesalers. Nike has rebuilt the relationship with its key wholesale clients through a new model of partnership, and the company is more involved in the product planning, marketing investments, category assortment discipline, and high-level in-store display. The initial and most impressive data that a re-engagement strategy is yielding results is the North American wholesale growth rate of 24% in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026.

The innovation engine of Nike has been restored, as shown in the 20%+ growth in the running category of the company in two quarters. Hill has focused investment in five sport "fields of play" including running, basketball, football (soccer), training, and sportswear. In January 2026, Nike introduced the Mind sneaker line, a series created on the basis of over 10 years of neuroscience-inspired materials science, and the Project Amplify bionic-powered performance platform.

Marketing spend by Nike, estimated to be around $5 billion in FY2026, is being used on branding, online platforms, and marketing activations. It directs the stories of athletes, cultural happenings, and the explosion of massive sporting events. Brand presence and commercial significance in sport-induced cultural events are extensive and culturally significant at Nike. In North America, Nike Digital also held zero promotional days in January and February 2026, compared to over 30 promotional days in January and February 2024.

The Path Back to $50 Billion: FY2027 Recovery Trajectory

Based on the agreement estimates, the turnover of Nike dropped to $46.3 billion in FY2025 as opposed to $51.4 billion in FY2024. The channel reset and inventory liquidation strain still took its toll on the top line, causing a slight decline to around $44.9 billion in FY2026. This is called the trough. The Nike revenue for FY2027 is characterized by a significant growth in categories and the recuperation of the expenditure in wholesale. Based on the Wall Street consensus of Seeking Alpha as of March 2026, the average analyst price target of NKE in 12 months is around $77.50. This is supported by the anticipation of a rapid pace of new product launches and an estimated recovery of revenues of about 8% in FY2027 as the channel inventories become balanced and the momentum in the wholesale gains momentum. Since revenue is likely to be near $50 billion within the near future and profits will resume to fall within the range of the bull-case ($85–$100), the most pertinent question is not whether a recovery will occur but rather when the EPS will recover enough to propel the margin growth beyond the current noise due to tariffs.

According to WallStreetZen's forecast across 41 analysts, the FY2026 EPS is projected to be approximately $1.59, which is lower than the $3.73 reported in FY2024 and higher than the earlier forecast. The majority of the costs were on tariff and restructuring, yet the growth in FY2027 was a huge 53% year-on-year. Consensus EPS values in FY2028 are more aligned at $3.11, which means that Nike still has a lot to regain in its earnings on a mid-cycle normalized basis. At the current price of approximately $53, NKE trades at roughly 22x FY2027E consensus EPS of $2.43—a meaningful discount to its historical mid-cycle multiple, reflecting the market’s continued caution ahead of the Q3 FY2026 earnings release. It is well positioned due to the quality of the brands under the holding franchise and the possibility of recovering EPS in FY2028. Analysts at TickerNerd report that the median price per share target in March 2026 is $84, which is an increase of 31% compared to the market. That is alright, but the bull case target of $120 (Oppenheimer/Brian Nagel) implies 87% upside from current levels.

Competitive Positioning: The Moat Competitors Cannot Cross

The best point against Nike in 2026 is that On Running and Hoka have stolen the market share of the brand, and this will never be recovered. It is a fine argument, but it fails to take into account the structural threat it presents to Nike and underestimates its competitive advantage. On Running and Hoka are two successful niche brands that have leveraged the consumer trend of maximalist-cushioning and Swiss-heritage aesthetics to sell products. Amazingly, they have grown in a real and impressive manner. The trailing twelve months revenue of On Running is approximately $2.4 billion, and it is under 5% of the current Nike annual run-rate revenue. Hoka, which is under Deckers Outdoor ownership, is similar in size and earns about $1.8 billion in revenue per year. The category leaders in the running market are not a major challenge to the dominance of a world brand with top standings in basketball, football, and training. On and Hoka are not concerned about sportswear.

The partnership between Nike and their athletes is too valuable to forgo. The Jordan Brand, a semi-autonomous unit of Nike, generates Nike about $6–$7 billion in yearly income—more income annually than On Running and Hoka together. The LeBron James, Cristiano Ronaldo, and Serena Williams franchises are powerful long-term assets that have helped create brand equity in various product lines. Nike is investing heavily in the FIFA World Cup 2026 (US and Canada), NBA Finals, and Champions League 2026. Few, if any, of the rival sporting brands have been able to utilize global sporting events on a scale with equal commercial success.

Share Price Context: Maximum Pessimism, Asymmetric Upside

The share price of NKE is an indicator of how the investors feel about the company, rather than the real business activities. The stock fell from approximately $73 in March 2025 to a 52-week low of $52.28, then recovered to approximately $68 following the Q2 FY2026 earnings beat, only to retrace further to the current $53 area as the market focused on forward margin guidance and the tariff environment. This is aligned with a stock that is at an early phase of recovery, and the indication of recovery is still present, but the link is not visible at once, and hence the highest negative correlation between price and intrinsic value. The current market capitalization, expressed as a valuation of approximately $82 billion, undervalues Nike by a large fraction of the long-run cash flow value, particularly given that the liquid assets on the balance sheet reflect a value of $8.35 billion and capital expenditure needs of less than sub-$5 billion annually.

The insider buying signal is particularly meaningful in this context. Elliott Hill, the CEO, had used $1 million dollars to buy NKE shares at prices in the $60s range. Another sign of commercially astute leadership in the company was the decision of its corporate executive chairman, Tim Cook to acquire NKE shares in late 2025. The long-term investors include Coldstream Capital Management, which has raised its interest in NKE by a half, and Exchange Traded Concepts LLC, which has raised its share by nearly 700%, according to the SEC Form 13F filings made in Q3 2025. An increase of 33% in the ownership of Soros Fund Management. The past has been determined to have a correlation between institutional purchases at the current levels and future returns above average.

Risk Factors That Merit Consideration

Greater China Structural Weakness

The Greater China business, historically, generating an estimated 15% of the annual revenue of the company, fell by 16% every year in the sixth consecutive quarter of Q2 FY2026. The reason why the Chinese domestic athletic footwear and apparel market has been going through a boom is because of the patriotic consumerism, lower price, and sponsoring sportsmen. The revelation of CEO Hill is that Greater China is grappling with structural difficulties in the market place, which will take time to overcome, and the FY2026 outlook of the third quarter promises to lead to an ongoing drag. Hill has called China a "big opportunity" in the long term and the near-term direction is uncertain and requires monitoring.

Tariff Headwind Magnitude

The incremental product cost of about $1.5 billion per annum in U.S. tariffs, about 315 basis points of gross margin headwind prior to mitigation, is a material cost that will continue to agitate throughout fiscal 2026. The suggested rebates in the supply chain to non-tariffed manufacturing facilities and price increments are likely to lower the net effect to just around 120 basis points, although supply chain transition is a very complicated process that may lead to delays. An increase in U.S. trade policy beyond current tariff levels would be an added strain to the recovery timeline.

Converse Brand Deterioration

Nike's Converse sub-brand --- historically a high-margin, culturally relevant sneaker franchise generating approximately $2 billion in annual revenue --- declined 30% in Q2 FY2026, representing a collapse that is more severe than channel cleaning or promotional repression. The Chuck Taylor All Star shape has remained the same over decades, and the Converse challenge poses a big threat to the traditional customers of the brand, consisting of young consumers. More fashion-conscious urban consumers have begun to move towards New Balance and other brands that are more culturally oriented. The lack of a clear public strategy is an issue with Converse.

Execution Risk During Multi-Year Reset

Meanwhile, Nike is forced to deal with the liquidation of its warehouse, the reconstruction of its wholesale channel, the reorganization of its supply chain, the development of a product innovation program, and the reorganization of its 8,000-strong organization. Implementing these measures may be difficult because the implementation of these actions may be resource constrained due to management bandwidth or other unforeseen external factors that postpone recovery when done simultaneously. The announcement of a restructuring fee of $300 million and the organizational realignments take care of the expense and the disturbance of repositioning a company like Nike at the pace that Hill saw it.

Bull and Bear Cases

The tariff environment stabilizes or even turns around, and Greater China becomes more stable in the second half of fiscal 2026. In accordance with such projections, the FY2027 earnings would grow from $2.43 to about $3.50 in FY2028 when the gross margins would revive to the range of 43--44%. A 24x forward multiple of FY2028E EPS would imply a share price of between $84--$90 by the end of 2027, with a margin recovery rate higher than expected provided it is above $100+. The next key landmark is the March 2026 Q3 earnings announcement: if gross margins improve sequentially or if China provides a favorable commentary, a significant re-rating is expected.

In the bear scenario, it is not possible to stabilize China, and then no meaningful steps can be made to bring about a reduction in the tariff burden and retardation of wholesale growth will be experienced when the channel-restock enthusiasm declines. The increase in the level of revenues at the trough in FY2026, the fall in the EPS below the consensus in FY2027, and the trough valuation of 20x decreasing earnings—between $48 and $52—show that investors are terrified by the structural defect of On and Hoka among others. Such a bear situation suggests that local Chinese brands have a more sustainable competitive challenge than the management considers at the current time. Bear conditions mean that there must be several parallel failures in the turnaround, and mean a level of brand decay that cannot yet be detected in the market share figures of Nike in the critical categories world-wide.

Conclusion: The Case for Strong Buy

Nike is not broken. The Air Jordan team has invented the science of performance footwear, and invented the most recognizable sports brand of all time, which has earned it up to $51 billion in revenue as recently as FY2024. The macroeconomic environment and three years of miscalculation strategies were extreme, and yet revenue was greater than expected in FY2024. In the short-term, there is a decline in investor confidence, and this will result in the highest level of worst-case pessimism and a long-term, fundamental investor. Such a point of entry is available today, with a price of about $53 per share.

The 20%+ running category growth sustained over two quarters is indicative of genuine inertia of the products rather than a promotional sham. The 8% growth in wholesale revenues, which follows the previously determined channel de-escalation, may be regarded as the structural reorganization of the Nike retailing partnerships. Accumulation of insiders, growth in institutional ownership, and the size of Q2 earnings beat are supporting the recovery thesis.

My $85 price target is derived as follows: I apply a 24x P/E multiple to the FY2027E EPS consensus of $2.43 (per Seeking Alpha). The 24x multiple is lower than the historical mid-cycle Nike P/E of 28–32x which reflects the execution and tariff risk that remains. The forecasts show that the 24x forward P/E rate of Nike multi-decade compounded history, which has risen to FY2027E, has not slowed down. Normalized tariffs and improved gross margins (top-line growth of 53% YoY) increased the EPS figure of the company to $2.43. Possibility of multiple expansion provided that milestones of recovery are achieved; one-year horizon with no assumed buyback acceleration. The figure of $85 is an increase of approximately 60% of its present level of $53. Dividend yield stands at 2.6%, giving the topside return target of more than 63%. Strong Buy.

data.title: Community Trust Bancorp: Better Asset Quality, Cheaper Valuation (Upgrade)
data.description:
One bank that has recently outperformed my expectations is Community Trust Bancorp (CTBI). Back in December of last year, I made the decision to downgrade the stock from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’. During the time that I had been bullish on it, the business had performed more or less along the lines of the broader market. But since downgrading it, it has risen 4.2% while the S&P 500 is down 1%. From a purely fundamental standpoint, the institution is doing quite well. Revenue and profits are rising as net interest margin expands. Asset quality is strong, and the stock is not exactly expensive. At the end of the day, I am rather impressed with what I see here. So impressed, in fact, that I think that upgrading it back to a soft ‘buy’ is logical here.

The picture has improved

Perhaps the most interesting metric when it comes to analyzing Community Trust Bancorp right now is its net interest margin. In the chart above, you can see what this was for 2025 compared to 2024, as well as for the final quarter of each of those years. You should notice a nice increase, which as the chart below illustrates, was instrumental in pushing net interest income for the institution up year over year. This rise, it is worth noting, was driven in large part by the fact that the Federal Reserve has been cutting interest rates.

You see, when rates are high, financial institutions often have to compete with one another for deposits. This means offering higher deposit rates. But as rates have come down, banks have been given cover to slash those rates. In the chart below, you can see certain key aspects of the balance sheet. The chart shows how time deposits as well as savings and demand deposits got cheaper for the company from 2024 to 2025. Long term debt has also dropped, though it does account for only a relatively small portion of the company's balance sheet. At the same time as this occurred, management was able to continue capturing strong returns from key asset areas. Loans inched up from 6.47% to 6.50%. And US Treasury and agency securities, which account for the largest chunk of its entire securities portfolio, grew from 2.13% to 2.36%.

Overall, this has resulted in positive results for the institution as I showed already. We have also seen a bit of an increase in non-interest income. The final quarter saw this metric come in at $16.6 million. And the year before that, it was $16.2 million. For the year as a whole, the $63.6 million generated by the company exceeded the $62.6 million reported a year earlier. These are not huge improvements, but they are improvements all the same. Largely, they seem to have been the result of continued growth when it comes to the company's trust and wealth management activities, with income they are growing from $14.9 million in 2024 to $16.8 million last year.

Management has actually done quite well when it has come to its trust assets under management. These went from $3.4 billion in 2023 to $4.1 billion in 2025. So it stands to reason that the company would see a rise in revenue associated with these actions. At the end of the day, this, working in concert with net interest income, pushed net profits up as well. This included the $98.1 million in net income generated for last year compared to the $82.8 million reported the year prior.

There have been other contributors to the company's expansion. Changes in the composition of the balance sheet can impact net interest margin. On top of this, an overall increase in the size of the balance sheet can improve revenue and profits as well. In the final quarter of last year, the institution had deposits of $5.39 billion. This is actually up nicely from the $5.07 billion reported the year before that. What's great here is that none of this was in the form of high cost brokered deposits. It is worth noting that the firm does have some exposure to uninsured deposits. Based on my calculations, 29.7% of all deposits were classified as uninsured last year. This is almost at the 30% maximum that I typically like to see.

As deposits increased, loans were able to grow as well. These expanded from $4.43 billion in 2024 to $4.83 billion in 2025. The greatest exposure here would be the $1.21 billion of real estate mortgages. Outside of that, no other category is worth $1 billion or more. The closest that we have would be the $959.9 million invested in commercial real estate that is non-residential, followed by the $862.5 million of indirect consumer loans.

Naturally, we should look at other parts of the balance sheet also. For instance, the company had $1.13 billion of securities on its books at the end of 2025. This compares favorably to the $1.06 billion reported the previous year. The firm also went from cash and cash equivalents of $73 million to $363.7 million. Meanwhile, debt inched higher from $308.4 million to $373.4 million. The picture that is painted here from these changes seems to suggest that management is focusing more of their capital on higher yielding opportunities like loans and securities. And they are willing to accept a little bit of additional debt, though that is offset by a larger cash balance that should protect the company to some extent.

If we use the results that the company achieved for 2025, we would see in the chart above that shares are trading at a price to earnings multiple of 11. That's actually just a bit above the 10 maximum that I prefer to see. It also means that the company isn't the cheapest candidate out there. But it is cheaper than three of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. In the chart below, you can see that it's even cheaper when it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. Only one of the five institutions is cheaper than it is in this regard.

One thing that is really attractive to me is the fact that asset quality for the institution is really quite high. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.63%. That is quite a bit above the 1% minimum that I look for. It also puts the institution above three of the five banks that I stacked it up against. In the chart below that, I did the same type of comparative analysis using return on equity. A reading of 12.71% is measurably higher than the 10% minimum that I look for. It's also higher than four of the five institutions that I compared it to. On this basis, the company is doing quite well.

This does not mean that the institution is not without certain risks. For instance, even though I mentioned that changes in the interest rate environment have been a positive for the institution, that is not always guaranteed to be the case. In the image below, you can see what changes to the interest rate would do to the institution over the course of a year based on results achieved in 2025. It's not a huge drop, but a decline in the interest rate environment would negatively affect net interest income. Seeing as how I believe that the US is heading toward a recession, and I expect rates to fall faster than most people do, I would argue that we could see some compression here in the near term. The last thing that we should touch on would be non-performing assets. Back in 2024, 0.59% of its loans were classified as non-performing. But by 2025, that number had dropped to 0.39%. I generally like to see a reading here of 0.50% or lower. So the institution does meet that standard.

Takeaway

Based on the data available right now, it is my belief that Community Trust Bancorp is more compelling than I previously gave it credit for. I would actually say that down grading the stock was premature. Asset quality has gotten better, and the stock is just a bit cheaper than it was back then. It continues to grow its balance sheet, and the changes in net interest margin I've been very bullish for it. At the end of the day, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ rating is justified.

data.title: Roblox: Remarkable Year Quickly Forgotten
data.description:
Roblox Corporation (RBLX) continues to slump as the market hits stocks in the software, gaming and media businesses due to AI impact fears. The AI gaming platform just completed an extremely successful year and the market has extrapolated slower growth into big fears. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish on the stock, but Roblox is still looking for a bottom.

Remarkable Year

The market sold off the immersive gaming stock despite the incredibly strong quarterly results Roblox reported all of last year. The company reported Q4'25 bookings surged 63% to reach $2.2 billion while the stock has collapsed since the end of October.

Roblox reported off the charts metrics beyond bookings as follows:

  • Average DAUs: 144 million, up 69% YoY
  • Hours engaged: 35 billion, up 88% YoY
  • Average MUPs: 36.7 million, up 94% YoY

The market got spooked on new AI coding tools from Google Project Genie and just overall fears from Anthropic AI coding prowess. Just about every expert has shot down these threats as the Project Genie prototype only generates 60 seconds of virtual video with no game play tools and requires a $250 monthly subscription.

On the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO David Baszucki made this statement about the lack of a threat:

We're building multiplayer platform technology. We're building stuff that brings people together. And a lot of the current work that you see out there is operating in video latent space rather than synchronize 3D multiplayer cloud space.

Roblox posted data on Twitter discussing 4D AI models that go far beyond what Google provides with Project Genie.

OpenAI even just closed down the Sora video generation app, leading Disney (DIS) to cancel a $1 billion investment in the service. The move highlights the high costs of the AI video technology and complications of making AI videos and turning those into a profitable business.

At the recent Morgan Stanley Technology conference, CFO Naveen Chopra again reinforced the benefits of to an AI gaming platform on providing more tools for gaming developers as follows:

We see a world where these AI technologies will allow people to someday create games without having to write a single line of code. That translates to more creators and more content. And if you run a platform business like ours, that makes everything that we do from the discovery platform, the monetization platform, the safety infrastructure, the hosting infrastructure, the communication capabilities, the social graph, it makes all of those things much more valuable because you got more content and more creators.

A couple of months later, the data still supports AI providing a tailwind to Roblox.

Big 2026 Ahead

Roblox guided to 2026 bookings of $8.4 billion at the midpoint. The company doesn't foresee any problems with new AI video platforms leading to the guidance for bookings growing at a 24% clip this year.

In fact, on the Q4'25 earnings call, CFO Naveen Chopra highlighted why this bookings guidance is absurdly low:

...our bookings guidance does not assume because we wouldn't be able to predict it, another viral hit of the magnitude of a Grow Garden or a Steal a Brainrot.

In essence, management is guiding to normal annual business growth in the 20% range without any big hit. Surely, the company will start seeing viral hits on a regular basis leading to the upside potential similar to 2025.

The incredible part of the story is that Roblox originally guided to 2025 bookings of $5.25 billion and the immersive gaming platform is now targeting ~$8.4 billion for 2026. Remarkably, the stock is back to the same level when the booking guidance for last year wasn't so hot, yet Roblox expects to report at least 22% bookings growth this year after a monster 2025 that beat the original guidance by $1.5 billion.

Roblox now has a net cash balance of $4.5 billion, which makes the stock even cheaper from the $39 billion market. The stock trades below 5x bookings for 2026 and below 4x the bookings totals for 2027 that will ultimately top $10 billion.

The stock is still trying to find a bottom, so investors have to be careful loading up too soon. The stock market is fraught with AI fears and Roblox could fall to even lower valuation multiples.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Roblox is far too cheap. The stock is now trading at the same price as when the business was expecting over $3 billion in current fiscal year bookings.

Investors should use this weakness to load up on the stock, but one has to be patient for the momentum to turn around.

data.title: Girding For Higher Inflation
data.description:
The conflict in Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz for coming up on a month now. Ever since then, energy prices have been on a rollercoaster ride, reacting to every tidbit of news, rumor and speculation. Over 20 million barrels of oil transits this global checkpoint daily or did until late February before what should be viewed as a nascent regional war erupted.

This is a bigger disruption to global oil supplies than either what happened during Covid (where the lockdowns also reduced oil demand significantly) or the Kuwait invasion of 1990. Now, producers like Saudi Arabia have rerouted millions of barrels of daily exports to land pipelines. This has mitigated this bottleneck marginally. However, if this conflict continues it is easy to see oil approaching or surpassing the $147/barrel level it hit just prior to the Great Financial Crisis.

The good news is, thanks to the shale revolution, the U.S. is in a stronger position, as far as energy independence, than in 2008. This can be seen above. Europe is not so lucky and could face fuel shortages soon. Asia is in even worse shape as it takes 60% of the oil exports from the Middle East region, or just over 14.7 million barrels a day.

Still, oil is a fungible commodity to a large extent. And while there is a historical divergence between the WTI and Brent oil benchmarks, the American standard is still up sharply over the last month.

And obviously, this has pushed gasoline much higher, just over a buck a gallon as of the week ending 03/23. As or more importantly, diesel prices have moved from $3.50 at the end of 2025 to a current $5.38 a gallon. And the railroad and trucking industries run on diesel. That means the transportation cost for any good that has to be moved to its final destination, has moved up significantly. Jet fuel prices have roughly doubled and triggered some airlines like United Airlines (UAL) to boost ticket prices and put in plans to trim capacity. And this is on top of the long lines at major U.S. airports due to the non-funding of the TSA in the latest farce in D.C. that has resulted in another partial government shutdown.

And oil is not the only commodity that is highly dependent on transiting the Strait of Hormuz. Approximately one-fifth of all global LNG exports go through this chokepoint.

Significant Helium (needed for semiconductor production) and aluminum exports are dependent on this passage as well. Most importantly, huge amounts of nitrogen, urea and phosphate flow through this narrow strait. Combined with the choking off of natural gas outflows, this event has caused fertilizer prices to spike across the globe.

This will impact the U.S. less than Europe and Asia as our planting season is earlier and we have larger production volumes in these compounds. Russia also just suspended some ammonium nitrate exports from March 21 through April 21 to secure their domestic fertilizer supplies during the spring planting season. However, like oil, these commodities are fungible to a substantial extent. While not yet to the levels following the lead up to the invasion of Ukraine, key components of fertilizer have moved substantially higher. Fertilizer generally makes up roughly 20% of food production costs, which varies by crop. Lack of proper fertilizer can trim crop yields significantly as well.

These additional costs are going to push food inflation levels higher and with a considerable lag (6-9 months). Two other things need to be noted on the inflation front as well. First, inflation was already starting to rebound before things flared up in the Middle East. The February Producer Price Index or PPI came at 0.7% on month-over-month basis, which was considerably higher than expected.

Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon" - Milton Friedman

Finally, we have another trend that is getting little attention from the financial press that is going to play a part in pushing inflation higher in the coming months and quarters. And that is the Federal Reserve has their money printer revving up again. M2 money supply set a new record in February. It rose 0.9% on a month-over-month basis. This is the sharpest monthly increase since October 2021 and up 4.9% on a year-over-year basis. This is the sharpest annual increase since July of 2022. M2 money supply is now up 46.3% from pre-pandemic levels.

Investors came into 2026 with hopes for a couple of 25bps reductions in the Fed Funds rate on the horizon during the year. They were also counting on earnings growth broadening away from the Magnificent 7, which delivered the bulk of earnings growth from the S&P 500 from 2023-2025.

Unfortunately, the probability of rate cuts has fallen dramatically since the outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East. If this conflict continues to push energy and commodity prices higher, the next move by the central bank might be a hike in the Fed Funds rate.

And higher commodity and energy prices are going to crimp profit margins outside a few businesses like those in the energy sector. Not to mention reduce end-user demand. In addition, the free cash flow from most of the Magnificent Seven is plunging due to increased investments in AI infrastructure and building massive data centers.

There are several asset classes and industries already in official bear markets. However, the S&P 500 is down less than five percent year to date in 2026.

Given current market valuations and growing probabilities of Stagflation and other worries (private credit, CRE, potential AI bubble, deteriorating job growth, etc...), investors are demonstrating way too much complacency. Inflation is just one more issue that is likely to be a growing problem for both the U.S. economy and equity markets in the weeks and months ahead.

data.title: Credo: Alphabet Just Announced A Game Changer
data.description:
Alphabet/Google (GOOG / GOOGL) just announced yesterday (March 25) three sets of new algorithms that target intensifying memory bottlenecks in AI compute. At the forefront is “TurboQuant”, which is designed to compress the increasing key value cache (“KV cache”) memory footprints inherent in high-volume inference – especially agentic AI workflows – without compromising on performance and accuracy. TurboQuant is enabled by the “Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss” (“QJL”) and “PolarQuant” algorithmic components, which together form a software advancement that complements recent hardware designs aimed at addressing the memory bottleneck in the next phase of AI compute for high-volume inference at scale.

Google’s latest algorithmic advancements targeting AI memory constraints represent a key validating catalyst for Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd’s (CRDO) product roadmap, particularly its three most recent offerings – namely, Active Led Cables (“ALCs”), ZeroFlap Optics (“ZF Optics”) and “Weaver” OmniConnect gearboxes. Given the rapid scale-out (i.e., cross-server and cross-rack connections) of increasingly large AI cluster deployments, as well as intensifying memory constraints in the next phase of AI compute, Google’s latest algorithmic developments further underscores the need for complementary hardware advancements. Specifically, the shift underscores growing demand for efficient memory connectivity, as well as higher-bandwidth networking and more reliable optical interconnect alternatives capable of overcoming imminent infrastructure bottlenecks.

And Credo’s product roadmap is competitively positioned to address the emerging shift in infrastructure demand. This is expected to further reinforce the durability of Credo’s high growth and earnings accretive trajectory ahead of the secular transformation in AI infrastructure, which remains underappreciated at current levels.

Google’s New Memory Compression Algorithms

As discussed in my latest coverage on Sandisk (SNDK), the emerging inflection in agentic AI adoption is expected to further intensify current memory constraints. Specifically, agentic workflows operate in persistent reasoning loops that span task planning, execution and evaluation. This workflow inherently expands “context windows” – or the volume of data that must be stored within the compute systems and be readily retrievable to sustain inference tasks – which increases demand for AI memory and storage solutions.

Instead of having an underlying large language model, or LLM, to repeatedly reprocess prior tokens every time new ones are generated, current AI infrastructure are accommodated with software that stores the related context in a KV cache. Specifically, every time a model processes a new token from text prompts, the token is assigned a query, key and value. “Query” refers to what the token is looking for, while a “key” labels the information contained in the token and the “value” refers to the actual information contained in the token. The keys and values from prior tokens are then stored in a KV cache, akin to a cabinet of labelled folders of information, so the underlying LLM doesn’t have to recompute prior tokens for context every time a new one is processed.

This is especially mission critical in scaling agentic AI workflows, as persistent reasoning loops require the underlying LLM to continuously reference context from prior prompts to plan, evaluate and execute tasks. The KV cache essentially allows higher speed and more efficient processing of long prompts by reducing repeated compute, making it a critical component in scaling inference workloads.

KV cache requirements are poised to accelerate as increasing agentic AI adoption accelerates the expansion of context windows. This accordingly raises memory demand, reinforcing the bottlenecks emerging in next-generation AI infrastructure. From a hardware perspective, this dynamic explains the increasing need for on-XPU high-bandwidth memory (“HBM”) chips, which stores the most critical KVs for immediate token processing, as well as off-chip local memory (e.g., DRAM) and shared infrastructure storage (e.g., SSD) for less demanding KV context.

From a software perspective, Google’s latest algorithms represent a key advancement that can further compress KV cache, effectively reducing the burden across the memory hierarchy in AI infrastructure. Specifically, vector quantization has long been a well-adopted KV compression technique in AI, effectively alleviating KV cache bottlenecks by enabling faster data retrieval and lower memory costs. Yet the technique has an inherent trade-off as the extra steps involved in compressing KV while preserving its accuracy could generate incremental overhead, partially offsetting the intended memory savings while adding computational complexity.

Google’s proprietary TurboQuant data compression method addresses this inherent overhead trade-off by combining the QJL and PolarQuant approaches. Specifically, PolarQuant compresses KV cache vectors by converting them into compact “radius-and-angle representation” rather than a traditional linear coordinate format. As Google puts it, it’s comparable to “replacing ‘go 3 blocks East, 3 blocks North’ with ‘go 5 blocks total at a 37-degree angle’” when describing the position of a memory vector. This effectively enables more predictability and efficiency in how the data is mapped, further reducing memory and storage requirements, as well as related costs. Meanwhile, QJL further addresses the memory overhead pertaining to maintaining data accuracy and retrieval speed in the process of KV compression by using the “Johnson-Lindenstrauss Transform” mathematical technique. The technique essentially compresses the numerical representation of token key vectors to a single bit – either +1 or -1 – while preserving the vector similarity for accurate context retrieval by the LLM. This effectively allows for much lower memory consumption without compromising accuracy and speed in the LLM context retrieval process.

These algorithmic advancements effectively alleviate the intensifying bottleneck in KV cache and the broader memory-storage hierarchy in data centers, improving cost and performance efficiency in scaling high-volume inference for agentic AI workloads. This development also underscores the increasingly mission critical role of efficient memory connectivity, as well as reliable high-bandwidth networking alternatives in scaling next-generation AI infrastructure deployments, which Credo’s product roadmap is purpose-built for.

Implications for Credo

As mentioned earlier, Credo’s three latest product categories span ALCs, ZF Optics and Weaver – the first product of its OmniConnect gearbox segment. This aligns closely with emerging bottlenecks in AI infrastructure, where focus is shifting from compute-heavy training workloads towards the efficiency required to scale inference within constrained memory and power envelopes. The dynamic has effectively increased focus on optimizing memory utilization and networking traffic to minimize system idle time, and optimize overall performance- or tokens-per-watt at scale.

Weaver

Weaver currently represents Credo’s first OmniConnect gearbox product, which is expected to start production ramp in fiscal 2028. Complementing Google’s recent algorithmic developments, OmniConnect represents Credo’s next-generation interconnect – or AI infrastructure networking – architecture purpose-built to address intensifying memory bottlenecks in scaling high-volume inference.

Specifically, the OmniConnect architecture is designed to optimize near-compute memory connectivity. This has become increasingly relevant in inference workloads, such as agentic AI reasoning, where the XPU requires fast and reliable access to large pools of memory, such as KV cache, for token processing. The OmniConnect architecture integrates “ultra-efficient 112G VSR SerDes”, which are short-reach high-speed electrical lanes that facilitate efficient data movement over short, near-XPU distances – spanning compute to memory, die to die, and chiplet to chiplet interconnects. It also incorporates a “lightweight AXI framer” that repackages data from the compute for higher-speed transfer over the VSR SerDes links to the attached memory component. The combination effectively enables “high-bandwidth, low-latency and power-optimized connectivity between compute engines and memory – whether on-die, off-substrate, or across chiplets.”

Weaver will be the first product of Credo’s OmniConnect architecture. It’s a “memory fanout gearbox,” representing a hardware advancement aimed at further alleviating the memory bottlenecks currently observed in scaling high-volume inference workloads. Weaver is designed to sit between the XPU and the attached memory, executing the OmniConnect architecture’s objective in enabling high-bandwidth, low-latency and power-optimized memory connectivity. It does so by unlocking a 10x boost in beachfront I/O density compared to conventional low-power LPDDR5X memory, facilitating the AI accelerator’s access to larger pools of memory more efficiently when processing inference workloads. Weaver effectively supports incremental cost-savings in inference as well, given the technology enables 20x incremental bandwidth and density in low-power LPDDR memory to match intensive demands of AI workloads. The technology is also designed with longevity in mind, given its compatibility with current LPDDR5X attached-memory, as well as next-generation LPDDR6, directly targeting hyperscaler and enterprise end-markets’ increasing demand for TCO optimization.

Interconnect Alternatives

In addition to memory, high-bandwidth networking has also become increasingly critical in supporting the scale-out of large AI cluster deployments optimized for next-generation inference workloads.

As discussed in one of my previous coverages, copper has historically been the default interconnect in facilitating the transfer of electrical signals in cross-chip and cross-server communication. Yet increasingly large-scale AI infrastructure builds, which often span across multiple servers and even data centers, are exposing the speed, signal integrity and reach imitations of traditional copper interconnects.

Aside from optical/photonics alternatives, Active Electrical Cable interconnects (“AECs”) have emerged as an effective copper-based bridge solution. Specifically, AECs can extend traditional copper interconnects to reaches of up to 7 meters, making it highly relevant in short-reach scale-out alternative to optical fabrics that may prove too powerful and expensive. While AECs currently represent a core growth driver of Credo’s, the company’s also recently introduced ALCs to further extend its reach into the TAM for AI infrastructure interconnects.

MicroLED ALCs

Specifically, ALCs represent Credo’s proprietary mid-reach optical solution that overcomes the inherent reach limitations of AECs, while maintaining similar cost efficiencies. The technology is enabled by the combination of Credo’s connectivity architecture with proprietary microLED technology recently integrated from the acquisition of Hyperlume.

Specifically, Hyperlume’s microLED technology was originally designed for scaling up chip-to-chip connectivity. It depends on “ultra-fast microLEDs and ultra-low power circuitry to overcome the energy and bandwidth bottlenecks inherent in traditional electronic interconnects.” This technology has then been integrated into Credo’s connectivity architecture, creating microLED ALCs – an optical-based alternative to AECs that can extend up to 30 meters of reach, while eliminating the reliability, power and cost disadvantages of traditional pluggable optics.

Similar to Weaver, Credo’s microLED ALCs are scheduled to start sampling in fiscal 2027, with initial shipments beginning to ramp in fiscal 2028. The go-to-market timeline would align with current industry expectations for further relevance of direct copper connects (“DACs”), such as AECs, in scale-up (i.e., chip-to-chip interconnects) and shorter-reach scale-out connectivity over the near-term due to their competitive unit economics.

...you really want to connect XPUs to XPUs directly where you can. And the best way to do that is to use direct attach copper. That's the lowest latency, lowest power, and lowest cost. So you want to keep doing that, especially in scale-up, as long as possible.

Source: Broadcom Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

ZeroFlap Optics

In addition to MicroLED ALCs, ZF Optics represent another one of Credo’s latest offerings targeting the transition to optical interconnects in AI infrastructure scale-out. Specifically, ZF Optics is Credo’s proprietary optical transceiver product used in end-to-end optical interconnects to facilitate the transmission and reception of data across server-to-server and rack-to-rack communications.

As discussed in a previous coverage, optical transceivers are critical components in optical interconnects. Each interconnecting fiber lane requires two transceivers – one on each side. The transceivers are equipped with laser and photodiodes, amongst other key components needed for the transmission/reception of data over optical links. The laser converts electrical signals from the compute in servers and switches into light for transmission across the fiber lanes. Meanwhile, the photodiodes receive and convert the light back to electrical signals for processing by the compute in servers and switches.

Credo’s ZF Optics is differentiated by its seamless integration with the company’s proprietary PILOT (“Predictive Integrity, Link Optimization, and Telemetry”) software and ZeroFlap Host SKD for “telemetry harvesting and coordination.” The hardware-software combination enables real-time monitoring and diagnostics, optimizing detection and mitigation of potential “link-flap” events – or any kind of instability and disruption in the interconnect – in the AI infrastructure’s network fabric. This effectively reinforces reliability, which is pinnacle to ensuring compute uptime and “enhancing AI cluster productivity” at scale.

Credo’s ZF Optics have already qualified with four customers across both the hyperscaler and neocloud end-markets. The product is expected to ramp extensively starting the first quarter of fiscal 2027, reinforcing Credo’s share in the emerging optical interconnect opportunity as large AI cluster deployments scale.

Fundamental Considerations

Based on the foregoing analysis on recent industry events and the implications on Credo’s roadmap, the company’s high growth and earnings accretive trajectory remains well intact. This is reinforced by Google’s latest advancement in addressing imminent bottlenecks of the next phase of AI adoption, which validates the increasing relevance of Credo’s next-generation AI infrastructure roadmap.

Base Case Scenario

Looking ahead, I expect the company to deliver a five-year revenue CAGR of 25% from fiscal 2026 to fiscal 2031, reflecting expectations of continued share gains as its growth broadens beyond AEC interconnects into emerging opportunities in optics/photonics. This is expected to reinforce durability of its long-term non-GAAP gross margin target of 63% to 65%, complementing ensuing operating leverage improvements in driving sustained FCF accretion at scale.

Upside Scenario

In the upside scenario, I expect the company to deliver a five-year revenue CAGR of 29% from fiscal 2026 to 2031. This reflects Credo’s incremental opportunity in solving intensifying AI memory constraints with its upcoming Weaver product based on the OmniConnect architecture. I believe this product spans beyond Credo’s core scale-up and scale-out interconnect business by targeting memory connectivity more precisely, which effectively broadens the company’s TAM from compute- and infrastructure-focused interconnects. The product’s upcoming go-to-market also aligns opportunistically with the transition to high-volume inference workloads, accelerated by growing agentic AI adoption, which will heighten demand for scalable and reliable memory connectivity, as discussed in the foregoing analysis.

This growth assumption is accompanied by anticipated operating income and net income expansion at a 39% and 34% CAGR, respectively, between fiscal 2026 and 2031. The assumption reflects a durable trajectory of sustained margin expansion at scale, reinforced by incremental growth opportunities from Credo’s upcoming OmniConnect portfolio in addressing increasingly urgent memory connectivity demands.

Price Considerations

Taken together, Google’s latest advancement represents incremental validation to the increasing strategic relevance of Credo’s product roadmap. This is expected to bolster Credo’s growth and FCF accretion prospects, skewing its valuation prospects toward my upside scenario projection of $125 per share.

The price target is derived under the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) approach, which analyzes the cash flow projections based on the upside scenario fundamental forecast discussed above.

A terminal value of $27.7 billion, which is determined by the application of a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on projected FY 2031 EBITDA, is considered. The 3.5% perpetual growth assumption imitates the higher-end of anticipated long-term economic expansion across Credo’s core markets, and reflects the company’s sustainable cash flow accretion runway at steady-state – proxied by terminal EBITDA – as its high-growth AI opportunities scale over time.

Total cash flow projections, including the terminal value, are discounted at a 10.3% WACC, which is in line with Credo’s debt-free capital structure and risk profile.

Risk Considerations

Despite Credo’s durable growth and earnings accretion outlook, reinforced by the strategic relevance of its product roadmap amid recent industry developments, it remains exposed to several sensitivities that could weigh on the stock’s near-term upside.

This includes Credo’s inherent revenue concentration. Specifically, it’s top three customers currently represent 88% of its quarterly revenues. While the company was previously expecting a fourth hyperscaler customer to generate greater than 10% of its revenues in fiscal Q3, this was absent due to the sheer size of upside driven by its top three customers.

In fact, our top 3 customers all grew sequentially from Q2 to Q3. So that really drove that growth. And our largest 3 customers in Q3 were also our largest in Q2, as you would expect but in a different order. Let me just talk briefly about our largest customer. They were 39% of revenue, and they were also the same customer that was our largest customer in Q1. So that was quite a large increase quarter-to-quarter for them. The second largest customer was 32% and they were our largest customer last quarter. And then finally, our third 10% customer was 17% of revenue, and that was the first hyperscaler that we had to ramp.

Source: Credo Q3 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

This dynamic leaves the company highly sensitive to any risk of revenue disruption at a single customer, while also increasingly its exposure to cyclical volatility in the event of technological and/or demand shifts.

The risk is further compounded by Credo’s inherent sensitivity to the current industry transition to optical interconnects in the ongoing scale-out of increasingly large AI cluster deployments. This is corroborated by the stock’s intraday decline of almost 15% on March 2 following Nvidia’s (NVDA) investment into optical/photonics pureplays Lumentum (LITE) and Coherent (COHR). This was then swiftly reversed with an intraday upsurge of almost 12% on March 5 following Broadcom’s (AVGO) fiscal Q1 earnings update, which advocated for the extended use of copper interconnects where possible in the ongoing scale-up and scale-out of AI infrastructure, which I’ve discussed earlier. This continues to underscore the Credo stock’s heightened exposure to risks of volatility as the shift in interconnect requirements for scaling large AI cluster deployments develop.

Final Thoughts

Taken together, Google’s latest algorithmic advancement targeting intensifying AI memory constraints validates the next focus area of AI infrastructure spending, which is poised to prioritize efficiency in scaling inference. This effectively provides reinforcement to Credo’s competitive product roadmap, which is positioned to expand from current AECs towards optical interconnects with its next-generation MicroLED ALCs and ZF Optics. The company’s prospective revenue share in next-generation AI infrastructure deployments is further supported by its targeted focus in memory connectivity hardware as well through the introduction of the OmniConnect architecture and its first Weaver product coming fiscal 2028.

The broadening portfolio is expected to strengthen Credo’s relevance in scaling next-generation AI infrastructure deployments, especially as scale-out network fabrics transition from traditional copper interconnects toward optical/photonics interconnects to address bottlenecks in latency, bandwidth and server-to-server and rack-to-rack reach. Given increasing alignment in Credo’s roadmap with end-market demand dynamics amid the secular AI transformation, the stock remains well-positioned for further upside from current levels.

data.title: Victoria's Secret: Bull Case Is Playing Out Nicely
data.description:
Investment action

I upgraded to a buy rating for Victoria's Secret & Co. (VSCO) previously, as it has continued to take share in the core intimates business, PINK is working out nicely, and international initiatives are scaling faster than I thought. Since early this year, things have progressed well. The core intimates business is still taking share, the customer file is improving, and international is scaling through a model that should allow growth to continue. I reiterate a buy rating.

The core business is showing proof that the turnaround is real

My previous thesis was anchored on the idea that the core intimates business was stabilizing, but fast forward to today, I think there is proof that it is doing much better, and more importantly, it is broader. The pushback previously was that any form of improvement was due to a few product wins or easier y/y comps, and I think the recent numbers we have should put that debate to rest.

First of all, VSCO continued to win total intimates market share for the third consecutive quarter, with share up low single digits. This is a strong signal that customers are choosing VSCO more often. Note that this is a mature category, and winning market share is not easy unless product relevance is actually improving. Secondly, the Victoria's Secret bra business grew mid-single digits in the latest Q4 and had finally seen annual growth for the first time since 2021. This second point is a huge one because, for VSCO, bras are the key growth driver for this business. You can think of VSCO's bras segment as the main funnel for its other assortment. When bras are working, the traffic and halo effect spreads into other categories. For instance, if a consumer goes into a VSCO store because of an attractive-looking bra, chances are they may be attracted to a similarly designed/concepted panty. That is exactly what happened here. Demand for panties accelerated and delivered their best performance since 2021.

The customer base is improving

This is another important part that makes the bull case more exciting. Management said overall customer count grew at a low single-digit rate, driven primarily by new customer acquisition, including younger customers. Retention among existing customers also improved, and spend per customer went up mid-single digits. These datapoints tell us a lot of important things at once.

  1. VSCO is capturing fresh demand (new customers). This is important because a turnaround does not last very long if growth comes only from the same customer base.
  2. The existing customer base is not churning at the same pace. This makes the recovery look more sustainable and also current product assortment is not alienating the core base.
  3. Existing customers are spending more. This suggests better product relevance and should translate to higher margins.
  4. VSCO managed to address one of the most important bearish debates, that it may lose brand relevance over time (an aging customer base). Clearly, that is not true as younger customers are joining the customer base.

If we look at the app data, it adds another bullish layer on this point. App downloads have increased 25% in the latest quarter, and apps now drive about one-third of digital sales. This is a sizeable mix of the business and shows that VSCO is creating a more direct customer relationship, which paves the way for VSCO to communicate new products faster, personalize offers better, and keep customers more engaged with the brand.

International momentum

The momentum outside of North America shouldn't be overlooked either. As a recap, in Q4 2025, international sales grew 43% (significantly faster than consolidated). While that figure benefited from the shift of European Union digital sales into the international channel, even if we adjusted for that, international still grew by 27%. I think one important component of the growth equation here is the way it is structured. Internationally, the setup includes franchise stores and digital channels, wholesale travel retail, joint ventures in China and the UK, and also a company-owned digital site that fulfills orders outside the US.

There are some operational risks with this type of model (lesser control, for example), but the reward is the pace of expansion can be much faster (operationally). Operationally, VSCO does not need to build every market by itself from day one. The local partners can help to open stores, operate digital channels, and, most importantly, help to execute locally. The last point is incredibly important because of different cultures, trends, and consumer preferences. This should allow VSCO to expand more aggressively into more markets at the same time.

In other words, I think this setup gives VSCO more ways to grow: either through a partner-led store expansion, through wholesale and royalty streams, through joint ventures, or even through its own digital channel where it sees direct demand. Longer-term, this international segment should gradually help to balance out the heavy dependence on the North American market, which should justify a higher valuation multiple.

Valuation

For a business that is executing extremely well and beating latest consensus estimates in its Q4 earnings, the sharp contraction in valuation multiples is not warranted in my view. VSCO now trades at ~12.5x forward PE vs. ~21x previously. Forward earnings growth expectations are actually better than during my previous update, with consensus now expecting mid-to-high-teen adjusted EPS growth through FY28 (Bloomberg's FY29 is actual FY28). The average multiple of peers is ~18.5x with an earnings growth outlook of ~10% for the next two years. Comparing it this way, VSCO deserves to trade at a premium, and I think ~18x is probably the right multiple since this is where Boot Barn Holdings (BOOT) trades at with high-teens earnings growth. Just from multiples re-rating alone, from 12.5x to 18x, that is close to a 50% upside.

Risk

The recent momentum may start to falter if VSCO fails to keep product newness and brand relevance high in core categories like bras and panties. I am positive on the momentum, but if management messes up on execution at this point, the entire turnaround story may come under heavy challenge by the bears. On top of that, as I said, international growth depends partly on franchise, license, wholesale, and joint venture partners, which means VSCO has less direct control over execution as it scales overseas.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for VSCO, as the bull case is playing out nicely. The core business is gaining share, bras are once again driving demand into adjacent categories, the customer base is getting healthier, and international momentum is strong. The stock is now only trading at ~12.5x forward earnings despite better forward growth expectations and strong execution. My view is that this disconnect is too wide, and if management keeps executing at this level, multiples should move up.

data.title: Henkel Buys Olaplex - Implications Are Negative
data.description:
I could, of course, have provided this article about 3 weeks ago, before the crash. However, because my recent article on Henkel (HENKY) was as little as 2 months ago, I thought it was over-ambitious to cover the company because of a mere surge in valuation. I was of the opinion, and still am, that my coverage at the time took into consideration the potential of not only the surge in valuation, but also the subsequent crash which we have been seeing since.
